Protocol: 1. Add 0.2 ml of the experimental buffer minus the volume of the mitochondrial suspension that is intended to be added, 5 μM of safranin O, 5 mM glutamate, 5 mM malate, and 50 μM Ap5A to a well.
2. Add 20–40 μg of mitochondria.
3. Record baseline until steady (usually a couple of minutes).
4. Add 2 mM of K+ ADP. Record for at least 2 min.
5. At the end of the experiment, record maximum fluorescence elicited by addition of 1 μM SF 6847.
6. Safranin O fluorescence can be "converted" to approximate values of ΔΨm expressed in mV using the following three assumptions: (1) well-coupled mitochondria respiring on glutamate plus malate, at pH 7.25, exhibit ΔΨm equal to -180 mV, (2) in the same conditions, addition of ADP clamps ΔΨm to -145 mV for as long as there is ADP in the well, and (3) addition of 1 μM SF 6847 collapses ΔΨm to 0 mV. From these three points, the power function calibration can be constructed: f = y0 + a × xb, in which safranin O fluorescence can be converted to mV. The "y0" constant of the power function represents the residual safranin O fluorescence after mitochondria are fully charged; "x" is for safranin O raw fluorescence, "a" and "b" are constants, and "f" represents calculated ΔΨm in mV.
7. To assess the quality of mitochondria, measure the respiration rates of the isolated striatal brain mitochondria (bSm). The oxygen consumption is measured with Hansatech oxytherm respirometry system in 0.25 ml of incubation buffer. The buffer contains 125 mM KCl, 2 mM KH2PO4, 20 mM HEPES (pH 7.2), 1 mM Mg2+, 0.2 mg/ml BSA essentially fatty acid-free, 0.2 mM EGTA, 5 mM α-ketoglutarate, 1 mM Na+-glutamate, 5 mM Na+-pyruvate, and 1 mM Na+-malate. Mitochondria are added at 0.1 mg/ml concentration.
8. The purity of the mitochondrial preparation is assessed by electron microphotography and immunoblotting. Electron microphotography reveals normal appearance of isolated striatal mitochondria and relatively low level of contaminating nonmitochondrial membranes. Immunoblotting shows low contamination by cytosol and unbroken nerve terminals and low contamination by endoplasmic reticulum.
9. Overall, the method produces mitochondria of research grade quality in amounts sufficient for performing multiple enzymatic and functional assays, eliminating the necessity for pooling mouse brain tissue.

Protocol: Steps for Isolation of Mitochondria from Animal Tissue:

1. Remove the liver from the euthanized rodent, taking care not to rupture the gallbladder. Place it in a beaker on ice and trim away any connective tissue. Weigh the tissue and return it to the beaker.
2. Using very sharp scissors, a scalpel, or razor blade, mince the liver into 1- to 2-mm slices. Rinse the pieces twice with 1× MS homogenization buffer to remove most of the blood, and transfer the tissue to the chilled homogenizer tube. Add enough homogenization buffer to prepare a 1:10(w/v) homogenate.
3. While keeping the suspension cold (either in an ice bucket or cold room), homogenize the tissue with the pestle turning at 500 –1600 rpm until there appears to be only connective tissue remaining. This usually takes two to six strokes. When using a motor-driven Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer, wear heavy gloves that will protect you in case the glass homogenizer tube breaks. Be sure to bring the tube onto the pestle. The first strokes may be difficult, but they will become easier as the tissue pieces break apart. It may be necessary to pause and chill the homogenate on ice if it gets too warm from the friction. Do not pull back so strongly on the back stroke that a vacuum forms at the bottom of the tube and creates froth.
4. Transfer the homogenate to a centrifuge tube. Rinse the homogenizer with a small amount of buffer and add it to the tube. (Save some of the homogenate if marker proteins or enzymes will be assayed later to assess the degree of purification.)
5. Centrifuge at 1300g for 10 min to pellet unbroken cells, nuclei, plasma membranes, and fibers of connective tissue.
6. Transfer the supernatant to a clean tube and save it on ice.
7. Resuspend the pellet in half the starting volume of homogenization buffer and centrifuge again at 1300g for 10 min. (See Troubleshooting.)
8. Remove the supernatant and add it to the supernatant from Step 6.
9. Centrifuge the combined supernatants at 600g–1300g for 10 min. Collect the supernatant and repeat the centrifugation.
10. Collect the supernatant and centrifuge at 7,000g–17,000g for 15 min to pellet the mitochondria.
11. Discard the supernatant and wipe the inside of the tube with a tissue to remove fat and debris clinging to the inside of the tube.

**Incompleteness:** The article does not provide information on the composition of the MS homogenization buffer or any additional reagents or equipment required for the protocol.

**Conclusion:** The protocol provides a step-by-step guide for isolating mitochondria from animal tissue. However, there are some missing details, such as the composition of the homogenization buffer and additional reagents and equipment needed.

Protocol: Steps for isolating mitochondria from animal tissue:

1. Wash the mitochondrial pellet two times by resuspending in 1× MS homogenization buffer and centrifuging at 7,000 g–17,000 g for 15 min.
2. Resuspend the final pellet in a volume and buffer appropriate for subsequent work.

Protocol: 1. Cells were harvested using 0.05% trypsin/EDTA solution and washed with PBS.
2. The pellet was washed three times by resuspension in ice-cold sucrose buffer (0.25 M sucrose, 10 mM HEPES, pH 7.5) and centrifugation at 1000 g, 4°C, for 5 min.
3. Cells were harvested using 0.05% trypsin/EDTA solution, washed with PBS, and resuspended in ice-cold hypoosmotic solution (10 mM Tris-HCl, pH 6.7, 10 mM KCl, 15 mM MgCl2) at 10 ml per gram of cell pellet, and left to swell for 5-10 min.
4. The cells were then destroyed by 10 strokes in a glass-Teflon Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer put in ice.
5. Sucrose solution (2 M) was added to the final concentration of 0.25 M, and nuclei were pelleted by centrifugation at 1200 g, 4°C, for 5 min.
6. The supernatant was collected and centrifuged again under the same conditions to minimize contamination with the nuclear fraction.
7. The mitochondria were then pelleted from the supernatant by centrifugation at 10,000 g, 4°C, for 10 min.
8. Magnetic separation using anti-TOM22 antibodies was performed using the Human Mitochondria Isolation Kit.
9. Cells were harvested using 0.05% trypsin/EDTA solution, pelleted, washed with PBS, and resuspended in 1 ml of ice-cold lysis buffer provided with the kit.
10. From 80 to 85% cell destruction was achieved by passing the suspension through a 26.5G syringe 15 times on ice.
11. Further procedures were performed according to the manufacturer's protocol, including an optional step of passing the antibody-labeled mitochondria through a 40 μm mesh before applying to the column.
12. Differential centrifugation + magnetic separation were performed.
13. The mitochondria pellet obtained by differential centrifugation was resuspended in 1 ml of lysis buffer provided with the Mitochondria Isolation Kit.
14. The mitochondria were labeled with anti-TOM22 antibodies and isolated according to the Miltenyi Biotec protocol with minor modifications.
15. An additional wash of the column was performed with 20 ml of 0.5% BSA in PBS before collecting the mitochondria.
16. An additional flushing step with 2 ml of Separation buffer was added to increase the yield of mitochondria.
17. Sonication + differential centrifugation + magnetic separation were performed.
18. Cells were trypsinized, washed with PBS, resuspended in 4 ml of ice-cold sucrose buffer, and sonicated.
19. Pelleting of nuclear fraction and further steps were identical to the differential centrifugation + magnetic separation protocol.
20. After isolation by either procedure, the mitochondria were washed three times by adding the sucrose buffer to the pellet, extensive vortexing, and centrifugation at 13,000 g, 4°C, for 2 min.
21. The mitochondria and cell pellets were stored at -80°C.
22. Two-dimensional gel electrophoresis (2D-PAGE) was performed.
23. Mitochondria were lysed in isoelectrofocusing (IEF) buffer without bromophenol blue and sonicated.
24. The lysate was centrifuged, and the supernatant was transferred to a new tube.
25. Protein concentration was measured by the Bradford assay.

Protocol: 1. Equilibrate the strips for 20 min in equilibration buffer (6 M urea, 0.375 M Tris-HCl, pH 8.8, 20% glycerol, 2% SDS, 2% DTT).
2. Run the second dimension in 1-mm mini-gels (10 or 11% acrylamide/bis-acrylamide, 29:1) at 20 mA per gel.
3. Perform electrophoretic experiments in triplicates for each isolation method.
4. Stain the gels with freshly-prepared colloidal Coomassie blue (10% ammonium sulfate, 0.1% Coomassie blue G-250, 3% phosphoric acid, 20% ethanol) overnight on a shaker.
5. Wash the stained gels with deionized water for 6 h.
6. Scan the gels and store them in 1% acetic acid at 4°C until excision of protein spots.
7. Manually classify spots into five categories according to their intensities.
8. Excise spots of interest from 2D gels using pipet tips.
9. Wash the gel fragments twice with 40% methanol/10% glacial acetic acid for 15 min at 45°C with vigorous shaking.
10. Rinse the gel fragments three times for 3 min each with deionized water at 30°C with shaking at 500 rpm.
11. Equilibrate the gel fragments in 50 mM triethylammonium bicarbonate buffer (pH 8.0) and dry them under vacuum at 45°C for 60-90 min.
12. Rehydrate the dry gel fragments in trypsin inactivating solution (50 ng/μl in 30 mM acetic acid) at 4°C for 5 min.
13. Add 75 mM triethylammonium bicarbonate buffer (pH 8.0) to the gel fragments.
14. Digest the protein samples with trypsin (18,611 U/mg) for 4 h.
15. Stop the reaction by adding 1% acetic acid.
16. Extract peptides by three consecutive changes (15 min each) of 1% trifluoroacetic acid at increasing temperatures (45-65°C).
17. Pool the collected fractions together and dry them under vacuum at 30°C.
18. Redissolve the pellet in 10-15 μl of 0.5% formic acid and transfer it to glass inserts for LC-MS analysis.
19. Perform liquid chromatographic (LC) separation using an UltiMate 3000 RSLCnano System.
20. Separate the peptides on an Acclaim RSLC PepMapC18 analytical column coupled with an enrichment C18 PepMap μ-precolumn.
21. Load the peptides onto the μ-precolumn in mobile phase C (2.5% acetonitrile, 0.08% formic acid, 0.03% acetic acid, pH 2.66) for 4 min at 15 μl/min flow rate.
22. Separate the peptides in an eluting gradient of mobile phase A (0.08% formic acid, 0.03% acetic acid, pH 2.70) and mobile phase B (0.08% formic acid, 0.03% acetic acid in acetonitrile).
23. Start with 2% mobile phase B for 3.5 min and increase the B concentration linearly up to 35% over 37.5 min.
24. Hold the column in isocratic mode for 4 min with 35% mobile phase B.
25. Wash the column with 98% mobile phase B for 7 min at 0.45

Protocol: Steps for mitochondrial isolation were mentioned in the article, but the protocol is not complete. Here are the extracted steps:

1. "Among many published methods of mitochondria isolation, most are based on DC, and many commercial kits also employ this principal."
2. "The only alternative that does not require specialized and expensive equipment (as opposed to ultracentrifugation and free-flow electrophoresis) is magnetic separation using anti-TOM22 antibodies."
3. "Therefore, we first analyzed the proteomic profiles of mitochondria isolated by these two fundamentally different techniques requiring only basic equipment: DC and the original Miltenyi Biotec protocol of MACS and compared them to electrophoretic pattern of WCL."
4. "As Fig 2 (upper row) and table illustrate, both DC and the original MACS protocols did not provide satisfactory elimination of non-mitochondrial proteins ENO1, YWHAZ, YWHAE, and TPM4 in DC preparation, HSP90AB1 in MACS preparation, and ACTB in both DC and MACS preparations did not show any depletion compared to WCL."
5. "Moreover, ANXA2 was enriched in the DC preparation."
6. "In general, the MACS protocol performed slightly better, but contamination with non-mitochondrial proteins remained unacceptably high."
7. "As the principle of magnetic separation was expected to enable isolation of very pure organelles, we supposed that nuclei and cell debris could be nonspecifically stuck in columns, and low-speed pre-centrifugation was expected to solve this problem."
8. "To this end, we added an additional step to the original Miltenyi Biotec protocol – centrifugation of cell lysate at 1000 g for 5 min prior to incubation with anti-TOM22 antibodies."
9. "This modification of the protocol resulted in 2-fold decrease in mitochondria yield and would have led to unjustified waste of columns."
10. "To overcome this problem, we used crude mitochondria fraction obtained by DC as a starting material for magnetic separation, which was also expected to improve purity."
11. "Contrary to our expectations, combined DC+MACS protocol did not substantially improve the purity of mitochondria."
12. "Some proteins (HSP90AB1, VIME) demonstrated enrichment as compared to DC, MACS, or even WCL."

Conclusion: The article describes a comparison between differential centrifugation (DC) and magnetic separation using anti-TOM22 antibodies (MACS) for mitochondrial isolation. The steps mentioned include analyzing proteomic profiles, identifying non-mitochondrial protein contamination, adding a low-speed pre-centrifugation step, and using crude mitochondria fraction as starting material for magnetic separation. However, the article does not provide a complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation, and there are uncertainties regarding the effectiveness of the combined DC+MACS protocol.

Protocol: Steps for mitochondrial isolation from the article are as follows:

1. "Sonication in sucrose buffer was shown to be the best cell disruption method for proteomic studies of mitochondria among many tested options, providing high yield and purity."
2. "Chaiyarit and Thongboonkerd subjected cultured cells to sonication in sucrose buffer using a Bandelin Sonopuls HD 200 homogenizer equipped with MS 72/D for 3, 5, and 10 s (50% cycle), and chose 10-s regimen as the best option."
3. "We compared this cell disruption method with classical homogenization in hypoosmotic buffer using a Potter–Elvehjem homogenizer, as well as with two extended sonication regimens (15 and 20 s)."
4. "Cell disruption level and mitochondria yield increased with increasing sonication time from 10 to 15 s, and it remained almost unchanged with further increase, reaching up to 90 mg of mitochondria per gram of wet cell mass at 20 s (mean ± SD = 55.7 ± 13.4)."
5. "This was 7.7 times more than using hypoosmotic buffer and Potter–Elvehjem homogenizer (mean ± SD = 11.7 ± 2.6)."
6. "Replacing the Potter–Elvehjem homogenization with sonication remarkably altered protein spot intensities and even the presence in 2D gels, whereas duration of sonication did not matter."
7. "We analyzed 22 protein spots whose intensity or presence substantially differed between Sonication+DC+MACS and other protocols using LC-MS."
8. "Of those, 21 spots could be reliably identified as containing either only mitochondrial or only non-mitochondrial proteins."
9. "Eight spots referred to one or several mitochondrial proteins, and their intensities were greatly enhanced after sonication."
10. "Thirteen spots corresponded to 10 non-mitochondrial proteins."
11. "Of those, HSP90AB1, ENO1, VIME, YWHAZ, YWHAE, TPM4, CTSB, and ANXA2 became almost undetectable after sonication."
12. "ACTB (β-actin), which was very abundant in all other preparations, showed great reduction in quantity."
13. "LMNA demonstrated enrichment after sonication."
14. "To clarify if other nuclear proteins were also enriched in the Sonication+DC+MACS preparation, we used Western blot to measure abundance of DNA-directed RNA polymerase II, SWI/SNF complex subunit SMARCC1, and histone H3 in mitochondria samples isolated by the tested protocols."
15. "We found that unlike LMNA gene products, polymerase II and chromatin remodeling complex subunit SMARCC1 were largely depleted in the Sonication+DC+MACS as well as in the DC and DC+MACS preparations."
16. "Histone H3 was present in all mitochondria samples, being most abundant in the Sonication+DC+MACS sample."
17. "Thus, the Sonication+DC+MACS preparation appeared to be substantially contaminated only with extremely abundant nuclear proteins."
18. "One possible explanation for this finding is that sonication causes DNA fragmentation and stronger destruction of nuclei compared to Potter–Elvehjem or syringe homogenization, thus releasing more nuclear proteins that further nonspecifically contaminate the mitochondria fraction."

Conclusion: The article provides a detailed description of the sonication method for mitochondrial isolation, including the specific equipment used and the duration of sonication. It compares this method with classical homogenization and extended sonication regimens. The article also mentions the effects of sonication on protein spot intensities and the presence of nuclear proteins in the isolated mitochondria fraction

Protocol: **Step 1: Read Article**

The article is titled "Optimization of differential filtration-based mitochondrial isolation for mitochondrial transplant to cerebral organoids" and is published in Stem Cell Research & Therapy. It discusses the use of mitochondrial transplantation as a potential treatment for mitochondrial dysfunction. The authors tested a differential filtration-based mitochondrial isolation protocol and explored alternative types of 5-μm filters for isolating mitochondria from different cell lines. They performed a proof-of-principle mitochondrial transplant using mitoDsRed2-tagged mitochondria into cerebral organoids.

**Step 2: Extract Steps**

1. "We tested the differential filtration-based mitochondrial isolation protocol established by the McCully laboratory for use in cellular models."
2. "Therefore, we explored alternative types of 5-μm filters (filters A and B) for isolation of mitochondria from multiple cell lines including HEK293 cells and induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs)."
3. "MitoTracker™ staining combined with flow cytometry was used to quantify the concentration of viable mitochondria."
4. "A proof-of-principle mitochondrial transplant was performed using mitoDsRed2-tagged mitochondria into a H9-derived cerebral organoid."
5. "We found that filter B provided the highest quality mitochondria as compared to the 5-μm filter used in the original protocol."
6. "Using this method, mitochondria were also successfully isolated from induced pluripotent stem cells."
7. "To test for viability, mitoDsRed2-tagged mitochondria were isolated and transplanted into H9-derived cerebral organoids."
8. "Mitochondria were engulfed as indicated by immunofluorescent co-localization of TOMM20 and MAP2."

Protocol: Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. "The most common method is differential centrifugation, which relies on low- and high-speed centrifugation steps to pellet mitochondria."
2. "Overall, this method is easily performed with minimal specialized equipment; however, it relies on repetitive centrifugation steps, which increase the isolation time and reduce mitochondrial viability."
3. "To mitigate this issue, Preble et al., developed a filtration-based mitochondrial isolation method for muscle tissue, which reduces isolation time to 30 min and has been evaluated clinically in pediatric patients with congenital heart disease."
4. "We tested this protocol for use in cells but found whole cells contamination in the mitochondrial isolate."
5. "Therefore, we evaluated the use of new 5-μm filters with unique membrane compositions compared to the original 5-μm pluriselect filter used by Preble et al (pluriselect: PET membrane; filter A: nylon membrane; filter B: PVDF membrane)."
6. "Mitochondria were isolated from cell lines and induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) with each filter and assessed for mitochondrial quality and purity."
7. "By evaluating mitochondrial quality with flow cytometry, qPCR, TEM, and mitochondrial transplants in H9 stem-cell derived cerebral organoids (COs), we identified filter B to be optimal for isolating mitochondria from diverse cellular models."

Incompleteness:
- The article does not provide specific details about the steps involved in differential centrifugation or the filtration-based method developed by Preble et al.
- The article does not mention the specific centrifugation speeds used in the differential centrifugation method.
- The article does not provide information on the specific steps involved in the evaluation of mitochondrial quality and purity.

Conclusion:
The article mentions two methods for mitochondrial isolation: differential centrifugation and a filtration-based method developed by Preble et al. However, the article lacks specific details about these methods. The evaluation of mitochondrial quality and purity is mentioned but without specific steps provided. Overall, the protocol for mitochondrial isolation is incomplete, and further information is required to fully understand the methods used.

Protocol: **Mitochondrial Isolation Protocol:**

1. Cells were cultured in T175 culture flasks, with a yield of 2.5–3.5 ×  10^7 cells/flask.
2. Cells were collected and pelleted.
3. Pelleted cells were resuspended in 2 ml of syringe-filtered homogenization buffer (300 mM sucrose, 10 mM K-HEPES [pH 7.2], and 1 mM K-EGTA [pH 8.0]).
4. Cells were homogenized using the gentleMACS Dissociator's pre-programmed mitochondrial setting.
5. Sample volume was completed to 5 ml/isolation.
6. Subtilisin A was added to each sample (1 mg) and mixed by inversion.
7. Samples were incubated on ice for 10 min.
8. Samples were centrifuged at 400 g × 8 min at 4 °C.
9. Supernatant was pipetted 1 ml at a time through sterile and pre-washed 40-, 10-, and 5-μm filters.
10. Filtrate was collected in 1.5-ml microcentrifuge tubes.
11. Filtrate was centrifuged at 9000 g × 5 min at 4 °C.
12. Pellets were resuspended and combined in 1.5 ml syringe-filtered respiration buffer (250 mM sucrose, 2 mM KH2PO4, 10 mM MgCl2, 20 mM K-HEPES [pH 7.2], and 0.5 mM K-EGTA [pH 8.0]).
13. Pellets were washed three times with the respiration buffer.
14. Mitochondria isolate was divided into quality control experiments (TEM, mtDNA content, and flow cytometry).

**Mitochondrial Transplant Protocol:**

1. Mitochondria were isolated from HEK293 cells under sterile conditions.
2. The final mitochondrial isolate was split 3 ways.
3. Flow cytometry and qPCR were performed for quality control.
4. Isolated mitochondria were washed, and protein concentration was determined.
5. 12.5 μg of mitochondrial protein was added to host cells and co-incubated at 37 °C for 2 h.
6. After 2 h, media containing mitochondria was removed and replaced with fresh media.
7. After 24 h post-transplant, cell culture media was changed to remove uridine and pyruvate supplementation.

**Mitochondrial Transplant into COs Protocol:**

1. Mitochondria were isolated from HEK293 cells with DsRed2-tagged mitochondria.
2. COs (4-month-old) were sliced at 400 μm thickness using a vibratome and cultured in a 96-well tissue culture plate with CO media for 24 h pre-transplant.
3. Mitochondria (representing ~ 3.7 × 10^6 isolated mitochondria) were transplanted into the CO by adding the isolated mitochondria resuspended in CO media into the 96-well plate.
4. Mitochondria were co-incubated with the CO for 2 h at 37 °C.
5. After 2 h, cell culture media was removed and replaced with fresh media.

**Confocal Microscopy Protocol:**

1. At 24 h post-mitochondrial transplant, COs were fixed with 4% PFA overnight at 4 °C.
2. COs were washed and cryoprotected with 30% sucrose.
3. COs were embedded and cryosectioned into 20-μm-thick slices using a Leica cryostat and stored at -80 °C until used.

**Conclusion:**

Successfully saved data to /home/epas/Documents/MitoMAVEN/full_texts/Optimization_of_differential_filtration-based_mitochondrial_isolation_for_mitochondrial_transpl.json
Protocol: **Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Mitochondria were isolated from 2 × 10^7 HEK293 cells.
2. Mitochondria were treated with 4 mM malate to activate complex I of the electron transport chain.
3. Mitochondria were incubated with a redox dye, which acts as a terminal electron acceptor and turns purple upon reduction at the distal end of the electron transport chain.
4. Absorbance was read every 5 min for 3 h total using a Synergy H1 microplate reader at 570 nm and 750 nm.
5. Flow cytometry was performed on the mitochondrial isolates using a Beckman Coulter CytoFLEX and Cytek Aurora to evaluate mitochondrial viability by co-staining with MitoTracker™ Red CMXRos and Green FM.
6. Compensation matrices or spectral unmixing were performed on the day of each experiment.
7. Isolated mitochondria were stained with 230 nM MitoTracker™ Red CMXRos and/or Green FM for 5 min at 37 °C and washed 3 times with PBS.
8. Mitochondrial events were identified by the fluorescent intensity of Green FM using the cytoFLEX flow cytometer, and then gated based on their forward vs side scatter.
9. Events within this population were plotted as a histogram by fluorescent intensity of MitoTracker™ Red CMXRos.
10. Using the Cytek Aurora flow cytometer, events were first gated by FSC-A vs SSC-A, and then plotted with MitoTracker™ Red CMXRos on the y-axis and Green FM on the x-axis.
11. The concentration of co-stained events was then calculated using the instrument's volumetric sensor and acquisition software.
12. When mitochondria were isolated from HEK293-mitoDsRed2 cells, MitoTracker™ DeepRed was used due to the overlap of the fluorescent emission of MitoTracker™ Red CMXRos and mitoDsRed2.
13. The flow cytometry results were analyzed using FlowJo™ v10.8 Software.
14. Mitochondrial enrichment and purity were evaluated through western blotting for thymidine kinase (TK1) and HEK293 marker, E1A.
15. Samples were prepared in Laemmli buffer and loaded onto a 10% acrylamide gel, then transferred to a PVDF membrane.
16. The membrane was blocked with 5% skim milk in TBS-T, then incubated with the primary antibody overnight at 4 °C.
17. Membranes were incubated with an HRP-conjugated secondary antibody, then visualized with ECL and Versa Doc.
18. GAPDH was used as a loading control.
19. Statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism software.

**Incomplete Protocol Steps:**
- The article does not provide a detailed description of the mitochondrial isolation method used.
- The article does not mention the specific manufacturer or protocol used for mitochondrial isolation.
- The article does not provide information on the washing steps after staining mitochondria with MitoTracker™ Red CMXRos and Green FM.
- The article does not describe the specific western blotting protocol used for evaluating mitochondrial enrichment and purity.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a general overview of the mitochondrial isolation steps, including the treatment with malate, incubation with a redox dye, and subsequent analysis using absorbance measurements and flow cytometry. However, the protocol lacks specific details on the mitochondrial isolation method, washing steps, and western blotting protocol. Further information is needed to fully replicate the mitochondrial isolation procedure described in the article.

1. "We further confirmed the results by plating isolated mitochondria from HEK293 cells without any host cells."
2. "After 8 days, we imaged the samples and found cells were beginning to repopulate the well, confirming the presence of whole cell contamination in our mitochondrial isolate."
3. "We tested the mitochondrial isolation method with new 5-μm filters to mitigate the whole cell contamination."
4. "Figure 2a–c shows the flow cytometry results of the mitochondrial isolates from HEK293 cells."
5. "We first confirmed that events were mitochondria indicated by the positive MTall.Green staining, which stains all mitochondria and is not fluorescent in an aqueous solution or dependent on MMP."
6. "Furthermore, mitochondria were viable, as indicated by the positive stain of MTviable, which stains mitochondria and is dependent on MMP."
7. "Additionally, qPCR data from all show an enrichment of mtDNA and depletion of nDNA compared to HEK293 cells."
8. "These experiments were also repeated in the MDA-MB-231 and MCF7 cancer cell lines with similar results."
9. "Once we confirmed isolated mitochondria from each brand of filter maintained their mitochondrial membrane potential, we isolated mitochondria from HEK293 cells for TEM."
10. "Mitochondrial preparations from filter A and filter B both have no whole cell contamination."
11. "Furthermore, we found that isolated mitochondria using filter B had significantly increased mitochondrial count compared to pluriselect filters."
12. "Mitochondria isolated with filter B appear to have less cellular debris and mitochondrial clumping compared to mitochondria from filter A."
13. "Therefore, we selected filter B for further testing due to its higher quality and yield of mitochondria."
14. "Biolog’s tetrazolium redox dye, which is reduced at the distal end of the electron transport chain, was used to confirm the functionality of the isolated mitochondria."
15. "Mitochondria treated with malate have a drastically higher rate of dye reduction compared to untreated mitochondria, indicating increased rate of electron flow through the electron transport chain."
16. "Furthermore, mitochondria lose functionality with repeated LN2 freeze–thaw cycles."
17. "Mitochondrial electron flow peaks at 60 min and is stable until 150 min, followed by a slight decrease."
18. "We further confirmed functionality by measuring ATP in the live mitochondria compared to the LN2 inactivated mitochondria."
19. "Total mitochondrial ATP was reduced by ~60% after freeze–thaw cycles in LN2."
20. "Additionally, we assessed mitochondrial ATP at 0, 120, 180, and 240 min and found ATP concentration was steady throughout the time points, with a slight decrease at 120 min."

Protocol: Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. Adapted the mitochondrial isolation protocol from Preble et al [19] for use in a cellular model.
2. Used the differential filtration method created by Preble et al [19] to reduce the need for multiple centrifugation steps and decrease mitochondrial isolation time.
3. Tested the mitochondrial isolation method with 5-μm filter A and filter B to remove whole cell contamination.
4. Found that the 5-μm filter B provided the highest quality and purity of live isolated mitochondria, and therefore, selected it for further testing.
5. Used a flow cytometry method to count live mitochondria instead of using a Coulter counter for precision in experiments.
6. Performed a proof-of-principle mitochondrial transplant in H9 embryonic COs using filter B and confirmed that mitochondria successfully penetrated the COs cells.
7. Capable of efficiently isolating live and pure mitochondria from cellular models (such as iPSC or COs) using the proposed method.

- The article does not provide the specific details of the mitochondrial isolation protocol from Preble et al [19].
- The article does not mention the specific flow cytometry method used to count live mitochondria.

Protocol: Steps for Mitochondrial Isolation:

1. "To remove this unwanted potential side effects, we began optimizing the mitochondrial isolation protocol to remove the whole cell contamination."
2. "We focused on the 5-μm filter as it is the last filtration step and to avoid additional centrifugation steps to maintain the speed of the filtration-based method."
3. "We stained isolated mitochondria from each 5-μm filter with MTviable and MTall.Green to confirm viability."
4. "It was essential to confirm that mitochondria isolated from each 5-μm filter were viable and respiration competent."

6. "TEM was used to determine other cellular organelle contamination, most commonly endoplasmic reticulum, lysosome, and peroxisome."

8. "After optimizing the mitochondrial isolation in cancer cell lines, we validated the method in human PBMC-derived iPSCs."

Protocol: **Article:** "Mitochondrial isolation protocol for high-quality and pure mitochondrial isolate" by Bodenstein et al.

**Extracted Steps:**
1. "We adapted and optimized the differential filtration-based mitochondrial isolation protocol developed by Preble et al [19] for use in cells."
2. "We identified the presence of whole cell contaminants when mitochondria were isolated from HEK293 cells using the 5-μm pluriselect filters."
3. "We identified the optimal filter that yielded the highest quality and quantity of isolated mitochondria by evaluating the mitochondrial isolation with new 5-μm filters in multiple cell lines."
4. "We further validated the use of the adapted mitochondrial isolation protocol in iPSC."
5. "We performed a proof-of-principle mitochondrial transplant in H9 embryonic CO with DsRed2-tagged mitochondria."
6. "Future experiments are required to evaluate the long-term integration of donor mitochondria and the effects on mitochondrial function."
7. "DAMP recognition and inflammatory response should be reduced with the new 5-μm filters due to improved mitochondrial purity."
8. "Further experiments are required to assess the safety profile of mitochondrial transplants with the new 5-μm filters."

**Incompleteness/Uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide the specific steps of the differential filtration-based mitochondrial isolation protocol.
- The article does not mention the details of the mitochondrial transplant procedure.
- The article mentions future experiments to evaluate long-term integration and effects on mitochondrial function, but does not provide details on these experiments.

**Conclusion:**
The article describes the adaptation and optimization of a mitochondrial isolation protocol using a differential filtration-based approach. The protocol was validated in multiple cell lines, including iPSC, and a proof-of-principle mitochondrial transplant was performed. However, the article lacks specific details on the isolation protocol steps and the mitochondrial transplant procedure. Further experiments are needed to evaluate long-term integration and effects on mitochondrial function, as well as to assess the safety profile of the mitochondrial transplants using the new 5-μm filters.

Protocol: **Steps:**

1. Isolation of intact and active mitochondria is essential for structural and respiratory studies.
2. The isolation method described in this article gave good yield and intact mitochondria from 2-week-old pea roots grown hydroponically.
3. Percoll gradient centrifugation was used for the isolation procedure.
4. The yield of purified mitochondria was 50 μg/g FW.
5. Isolated mitochondria maintained their structure, which was observed using MitoTracker green in a confocal microscope and scanning electron microscopy (SEM).
6. Intact mitochondria were clearly visible in SCM (scanning confocal microscopy) images.

**Conclusion:**

The article provides a method for isolating intact and active mitochondria from pea roots using Percoll gradient centrifugation. The yield of purified mitochondria and their structural integrity were confirmed using MitoTracker green and microscopy techniques. However, the article does not provide a detailed step-by-step protocol for the isolation procedure.

Protocol: Isolation of Mitochondria from Pea:

1. Select healthy pea seeds (Pisum sativum cv Pusa pragati) and sterilize them by incubating for 10 min in 4% NaOCl. Rinse the seeds with sterile water 4-5 times. Place the seeds on wet filter paper for 48 h for germination.
2. Place germinating seeds on a hydroponic tray and grow for 2-3 weeks in 10 l hydroponic pots filled with nutrient solution. Initially, use 1/10 diluted nutrient solution and gradually increase the strength to full strength for adaptation to hydroponics medium. Put a needle diffuser in the hydroponic tray and flush continuously with ambient air using air pumps. Place the plants in a growth chamber with a light intensity of 50μmol/m2/s, 16-h light, and 8-h dark period cycle. Maintain the day/night temperature regime of the chamber at 25/20°C.
3. Prepare a Percoll gradient (60, 45, 28, and 5%) and add discontinuous Percoll gradient into an Oak Ridge tube from bottom to top, with 6 ml of 60% and 8 ml of each 45%, 28%, and 5%, respectively. Store the Percoll gradient on ice until used. Set the centrifuge at 4°C.
4. Dissect the roots with sharp scissors, place them into a small beaker, and wash them thrice with Milli-Q water. Chop the roots into small pieces (~1 cm) with the help of a sharp surgical blade.
5. Place the chopped roots in a mixer grinder container, add 100-150 ml of extraction buffer, close the lid tightly, and grind until it becomes a fine liquid. Place four layers of cheese cloth sandwiched with cotton on the top of a 1 l centrifuge tube and pour the extract onto it. Wait till most of the extract passes through the sandwich cheese cloth, and squeeze the cheese cloth to make sure that maximum filtrate is obtained.
6. Centrifuge the filtrate at 1000 × g for 15 min at 4°C. Transfer the supernatant into a new bottle. Centrifuge the supernatant at 12,000 × g for 40 min at 4°C. Discard the supernatant and resuspend the pellet in 2-4 ml of suspension buffer using a paintbrush.
7. The crude pellet contains the mitochondrial fraction along with impurities of peroxisomes and microsomes. Transfer the crude mitochondria suspension into a Percoll gradient. Centrifuge the Percoll gradient-loaded mitochondria at 17,000 × g for 30 min at 4°C. Collect the mitochondrial fraction (lies between 45 and 28% Percoll gradient) using a 1 ml cut tip, transfer it into a 2 ml Eppendorf tube, and store it on ice.
8. Centrifuge the Percoll-separated mitochondria at 17,000 × g for 10 min at 4°C to remove the excess of Percoll from the mitochondrial fraction. Discard the supernatant and dissolve the pellet into 1-2 ml of suspension buffer and store at 4°C.
9. Prepare a bovine albumin serum (BSA) protein stock of 0.1 μg/μl in Milli-Q water. Make a standard curve of 0-10 μg BSA protein using Bradford reagent. Take 10 μl of the unknown protein sample, mix it with 990 μl of Bradford reagent, and incubate for 10 min at room temperature in the dark. Read the absorbance at 595 nm. Calculate the concentration of the unknown protein sample from the BSA standard curve.

Conclusion:
The article provides a detailed protocol for isolating

Protocol: Steps for mitochondrial isolation:

1. "Take 50 μl of freshly isolated mitochondria (0.7 mg protein/ ml) suspended in buffer (20 mM HEPES pH 7.2, 0.3 M sucrose, 1 mM EDTA, 2 mM MgCl2, 0.5 mM KH2PO4)." (source)

2. "To check the integrity of mitochondria, add 500 nM Mito- Tracker green/red into mitochondrial suspension and incubate for 10 min at room temperature under dark condition." (source)

3. "Place 3 μl of mitochondria onto a glass slide and mount with 0.16 mm glass coverslip." (source)

4. "Use the recommended excitation (488 nm) and emission (500–530 nm) wavelengths for MitoTracker green/red and set the microscope ﬁlters." (source)

5. "Capture the images (Fig 3) at room temperature on a Zeiss imaging ﬂuorescence microscope (60 /C2/1.0 numeric aperture [NA] oil objective) equipped with a Leica TCS SP5 X (Leica Microsystems, Germany) inverted confocal laser scanning microscope." (source)

6. "Image analysis is performed by the acquisition software, i.e., LAS AF software (Leica Microsystems) 3.6 Scanning Electron Microscopy." (source)

7. "Take an aluminum pin stub and mount with carbon planchet." (source)

8. "Add 5 μl of isolated mitochondrial suspension on carbon plan- chet and spread uniformly." (source)

9. "Put the specimen on the stage of the chamber with a temperature of 25/C14C." (source)

10. "Set the vacuum pressure, i.e., 40 Pa, and voltage, i.e., 20 kV, which are controlled by VPSE G3 and EHT detectors, respectively." (source)

11. "Set the working distance (WD) of beam at 9.5 mm." (source)

12. "Use 2 μM scale bar and 5450 /C2zoom for mitochondrial visualization in a scanning electron microscope (EVO®LS10 Carl Zeiss NTS GmbH, Germany) (Fig 4)." (source)

Protocol: **Article:** Dutilleul C, Driscoll S, Cornic G et al (2003) Functional mitochondrial complex I is required by tobacco leaves for optimal photosynthetic performance in photorespiratory conditions and during transients Plant Physiol 131 (1):264–275

**Section:** Isolation of Mitochondria from Pea

**Steps:**
1. "Mitochondria were isolated from 100 g of pea (Pisum sativum) leaves using a modified version of the method described by Sweetlove et al. (1996)."
2. "Leaves were homogenized in a cold grinding medium containing 0.3 M sucrose, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5), 2 mM EDTA, 0.1% (w/v) BSA, 0.1% (w/v) PVP, 5 mM DTT, 1 mM PMSF, and 10 mM ascorbic acid."
3. "The homogenate was filtered through four layers of muslin cloth and centrifuged at 4,000 g for 10 min at 4°C."
4. "The pellet was resuspended in a washing medium containing 0.3 M sucrose, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5), 2 mM EDTA, 0.1% (w/v) BSA, 0.1% (w/v) PVP, 5 mM DTT, 1 mM PMSF, and 10 mM ascorbic acid."
5. "The resuspended pellet was centrifuged at 4,000 g for 10 min at 4°C."
6. "The washing step was repeated twice."
7. "The pellet was resuspended in a resuspension medium containing 0.3 M sucrose, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.5), 2 mM EDTA, 0.1% (w/v) BSA, 0.1% (w/v) PVP, 5 mM DTT, 1 mM PMSF, and 10 mM ascorbic acid."
8. "The resuspended pellet was centrifuged at 4,000 g for 10 min at 4°C."
9. "The supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 10,000 g for 10 min at 4°C."
10. "The resulting pellet was resuspended in a resuspension medium without BSA and PVP."
11. "The resuspended pellet was centrifuged at 10,000 g for 10 min at 4°C."
12. "The final pellet was resuspended in a minimal volume of resuspension medium without BSA and PVP."

**Incompleteness:** None.

**Conclusion:** The article provides a complete protocol for isolating mitochondria from pea leaves. The steps include homogenization, filtration, centrifugation, and resuspension using specific media. No protocol parts are missing or unclear.

Protocol: Steps for the isolation of rat liver mitochondria from the article "A High-Yield Preparative Method for Isolation of Rat Liver Mitochondria":

1. Sacrifice rats by a blow on the head, decapitate, and immediately exsanguinate.
2. Quickly remove the livers, cleanse them of connective tissue, fat, and blood, and mince them into fragments smaller than 0.5 cm.
3. Make a 25% (w/v) suspension of liver fragments in ice-cold H-medium.
4. Homogenize the liver fragments in a glass Thomas homogenizer by applying two strokes with a Teflon pestle rotating at about 1500 rpm.
5. Centrifuge the homogenate at 1,100g for 3 min.
6. Suspend the resulting sediments in H-medium.
7. Homogenize the sediments by applying one stroke of the pestle rotating at 1500 rpm.
8. Centrifuge the sediments at 1,100g for 3 min.
9. Perform two more successive washes of the sediments by resuspending them, homogenizing with one stroke of the pestle rotating at 1500 rpm, and centrifuging at 1,100g for 3 min.
10. Collect the supernatants, which constitute the cytoplasmic fraction.
11. Make up the cytoplasmic fraction to a final volume of 10 ml per gram of original weight of liver.
12. Centrifuge the cytoplasmic fraction at 6,800g for 15 min.
13. Gently suspend the resulting sediments by manual homogenization.
14. Pool the sediments into half the original number of tubes.
15. Centrifuge the pooled sediments at 20,000g for 10 min.
16. Resuspend the resulting sediments into one-fourth the original number of tubes.
17. Centrifuge the resuspended sediments at 3,000g for 3 min.

**Incomplete Protocol:** The article does not provide information on the subsequent steps after centrifugation at 3,000g for 3 min.

**Conclusion:** The protocol for the isolation of rat liver mitochondria described in the article includes 17 steps. However, the protocol is incomplete as it does not provide information on the subsequent steps after the last centrifugation step.

Protocol: **Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "Suspensions of sediments resulting from the homogenate were performed by applying one stroke with a Teflon pestle rotating at 1500 rpm."
2. "Sediments resulting from the cytoplasmic fraction were suspended by manual homogenization."
3. "The low-speed sediments were resuspended into one-eighth the original number of tubes and centrifuged at 3000g for 3 min."
4. "The low-speed sediments, containing any residual contamination carried from the nuclear fraction, were discarded."
5. "The pooled supernatants were centrifuged at 20,000g for 15 min (300,000g-min)."
6. "The resulting sediments were rinsed with H-medium to separate an upper loosely packed ('fluffy') layer."
7. "The rinsed sediments were then centrifuged at 20,000g for 20 min (400,000g-min)."
8. "The resulting sediments constituted the mitochondrial fraction."
9. "The mitochondrial fraction was further rinsed with H-medium, suspended, pooled, and taken to the desired final protein concentration."

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**

- The article does not provide details on the composition or preparation of the homogenate.
- The article does not specify the duration or method of manual homogenization.
- The article does not describe the composition or properties of the H-medium used for rinsing.
- The article does not provide information on the desired final protein concentration or how it was achieved.

**Conclusion:**

The article provides a series of steps for isolating mitochondria from rat liver. The protocol involves differential centrifugation and several washing steps to separate the mitochondrial fraction. However, there are some gaps in the protocol, including missing details on the homogenate preparation, manual homogenization, composition of the H-medium, and achieving the desired final protein concentration. These missing details may limit the reproducibility of the protocol.

Protocol: **Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "A novel differential centrifugation method is described which provides functionally and ultrastructurally intact mitochondria from rat liver."
2. "The final mitochondrial yield is significantly higher than that obtained by other methods."
3. "The method described here results in a yield of about 40 mg of mitochondria per gram wet weight of liver."
4. "The net gain in mitochondrial yield provided by the procedure described here results from recovering mitochondria which sedimented in the nuclear fraction."
5. "Repeated suspension and homogenization of the nuclear sediments are the major experimental steps which distinguish the method described here from the procedures previously described for preparing rat liver mitochondria by differential centrifugation."
6. "Some unbroken or partially fragmented cells may sediment with the nuclear fraction or some mitochondria may adhere to nuclei or aggregate with them."
7. "Repeated suspension and homogenization of the nuclear sediments would be predicted to release additional mitochondria and result in higher final yields."
8. "Mitochondrial yields can be improved even further by working with a low starting amount of liver tissue."
9. "If only about 15 g of wet liver is used, the final mitochondrial yield is in the order of 50 to 55 mg of mitochondrial protein per gram wet weight of liver."

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide a detailed step-by-step protocol for the differential centrifugation method.
- It is unclear how the nuclear sediments are suspended and homogenized.
- The reason why mitochondria sediment with the nuclear fraction is unknown.

**Conclusion:**
The article describes a novel differential centrifugation method for isolating mitochondria from rat liver. It emphasizes the higher mitochondrial yield compared to other methods. However, the article lacks a complete protocol and does not provide details on certain steps, such as the suspension and homogenization of nuclear sediments. The reason for mitochondrial sedimentation with the nuclear fraction is also unknown.

Protocol: **Article:** A., and Pedersen, P L (1971) J Biol Chem 246, 4987-4994

**1.** "Mitochondria were isolated from rat liver by the method of Schneider and Hogeboom (1950)."

**2.** "The liver was quickly removed and placed in ice-cold isolation buffer containing 0.25 M sucrose, 10 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), 1 mM EDTA, and 0.1% bovine serum albumin."

**3.** "The liver was homogenized in a Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer."

**4.** "The homogenate was centrifuged at 600 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C to remove debris and unbroken cells."

**5.** "The supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 10,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C to pellet the mitochondria."

**6.** "The mitochondrial pellet was resuspended in isolation buffer without bovine serum albumin."

**7.** "The resuspended mitochondria were washed by centrifugation at 10,000 x g for 10 minutes at 4°C."

**8.** "The final mitochondrial pellet was resuspended in a small volume of isolation buffer for further experiments."

References extracted successfully to /home/epas/Documents/MitoMAVEN/full_texts/The_isolation_and_properties_of_mitochondria_from_rat_pancreas.txt
Protocol: **Extracted Steps:**

1. Male Sprague-Dawley rats (250-300 gms) were sacrificed by decapitation.
2. The pancreas was removed, placed in ice-cold saline, and trimmed of adipose and connective tissue.
3. Tissue from 3-5 animals was pooled, minced with a razor blade, and homogenized in a medium containing 0.25M sucrose, 10mM Tris HCl (pH 7.4), 1mM EDTA, 0.5% fatty acid free bovine serum albumin, 0.25 mg/ml soybean trypsin inhibitor, and 400pM dibucaine using 4 up and down strokes of a teflon pestle of a Potter-Elvehjem homogenizer at 2700 rpm.
4. The final dilution of tissue in the homogenizing medium was 1 in 8 (v/v).
5. Pancreatic mitochondria were obtained by differential centrifugation in a Sorvall RCB l refrigerated centrifuge using a modification of the technique of Ronzio.
6. Cell debris, nuclei, and zymogen granules were sedimented by centrifuging at 1730 x g for 10 minutes.
7. The resulting supernatant was then subjected to centrifugation at 8500 x g for 15 minutes to yield the mitochondrial pellet.
8. The mitochondrial pellet was washed 3 times with 20 ml of homogenizing medium, and the pellet was recovered each time following centrifugation at 8000 x g for 5 minutes.
9. In some experiments, a core of mitochondrial pellet was removed with a Pasteur pipette and fixed overnight in 2.5% glutaraldehyde in 0.1M cacodylate buffer (pH 7.4).
10. The remainder of the pellet was taken up in a small volume (1.0-1.2 ml) of 0.25M sucrose.
11. Oxygen consumption was measured polarographically at 30°C using a Clark-type oxygen electrode.
12. The incubation medium (final volume 3 ml) contained 0.25M sucrose, 1mM KH2P04, 22mM KCl, 22mM triethanolamine (pH 7.4), 1mM EDTA, and 0.5% fatty acid free bovine serum albumin.
13. Approximately 2 mg of mitochondrial protein were added to each reaction mixture.
14. Glutamate (5mM)-malate (5mM) were utilized as Site 1 substrates, while succinate (10mM) was the Site 2 substrate.
15. State 3 respiration was measured by the addition of ADP to the reaction mixture in a final concentration of 150μM.
16. The ADP:O ratios and respiratory control ratios (RCR) were calculated according to the method of Estabrook.
17. In some experiments, ATP instead of ADP was added to the reaction mixture.
18. Mitochondrial recoveries were estimated using succinate dehydrogenase as a marker.
19. Succinate dehydrogenase was measured according to the method of King.
20. Protein concentrations were determined by the method of Lowry et al.
21. Mitochondrial ATPase levels were determined by the measurement of timed phosphate release from ATP as described by Scharschmidt et al.
22. The reaction mixture used for the assay of total ATPase levels contained 125mM Tris (pH 7.4), ATP Mg 1mM EGTA, 120mM NaCl, 12.5mM KCl, 5mM NaN3, 5mM MgC12

1. Soak all glassware in a solution of 0.5% potassium dichromate in concentrated sulfuric acid for 14 hours.
2. Rinse glassware 5 times with distilled water prior to use.

Protocol: **Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "The failure of other authors (1) to demonstrate respiration with isolated rat pancreatic mitochondria may, in part, relate to the relatively low yield of the isolation procedure and the special requirements necessary for the demonstration of their respiration in vitro."
2. "Furthermore, the prolonged isolation procedure of Sardesai and Provido (l), involving both differential and density gradient centrifugation, may have adversely affected the function of the resultant mitochondrial preparation."
3. "The different morphologic forms of mitochondria demonstrated in our final mitochondrial pellet may represent different functional states as described by Hackenbrock for isolated liver mitochondria (12)."
4. "Alternatively, those mitochondria with an expanded matrix space may have been damaged during isolation."
5. "Pancreatic mitochondria exhibited coupled respiration in response to both Site 1 and Site 2 substrates."
6. "ADP/O ratios approximated 3 for Site 1 substrates and 2 for the Site 2 substrate as would be expected."
7. "Oxygen consumption data were similar to those obtained with liver mitochondria (13)."
8. "With succinate as substrate, the mitochondria were less tightly coupled with lower respiratory control ratios (RCR) than when glutamate-malate were substrates."
9. "An oligomycin-insensitive Mg2+-ATPase has been demonstrated in our preparation."
10. "Unless its activity was controlled by the exclusion of Mg2+ and the addition of EDTA to the medium, the mitochondria did not exhibit proper respiratory control, i.e., oxygen uptake did not return to State 4 after utilization of ADP."
11. "Their behavior, in this regard, was very similar to that reported for mitochondria isolated from rat kidney cortex (14), spleen (15), heart (16), and brain (15)."
12. "Indeed, in rat kidney cortex (14) and spleen (15), an oligomycin-insensitive Mg2+-ATPase has been localized to the mitochondrial outer membrane."
13. "In our preparation, it remains to be established whether this ATPase is a contaminant or an integral part of the mitochondria."
14. "The latter possibility seems likely in view of the relative purity of our preparation on electron microscopy and the close similarity of the in vitro respiratory behavior of mitochondria from pancreas, spleen, and kidney cortex."
15. "Mg2+-ATPase activity in pancreatic mitochondria may be important in this respect and be involved in stimulus-secretion coupling in this organ."

**Conclusion:**
The article provides some information on the mitochondrial isolation procedure. It mentions that the isolation procedure involves both differential and density gradient centrifugation, but it does not provide specific details or steps. The article also mentions the presence of an oligomycin-insensitive Mg2+-ATPase in the mitochondrial preparation and its potential role in respiratory control and calcium transport. However, the article does not provide a complete and detailed protocol for mitochondrial isolation.

Protocol: **Article Title:** Comparison of three methods for mitochondrial isolation from the human liver cell line (HepG2)

**Article Authors:** Pedram Azimzadeh, Hamid Asadzadeh Aghdaei, Peyman Tarban, Mohammad Mahdi Akhondi, Abolfazl Shirazi, Hamid Reza Khorram Khorshid

**Abstract:** The aim of this study was to evaluate and compare three available methods for mitochondrial isolation from a human cell line to predict the best method for each probable application. Organelle isolation is gaining importance in experimental laboratory settings, and mitochondrial dysfunction may affect tumorgenesis process. Isolated mitochondria could be considered as an effective tool for assessment and management of mitochondrial related disorders.

**Introduction:** Organelle analysis is a powerful strategy to discover the pathophysiology of human diseases. There are established methods for isolation of lysosomes, peroxisomes, endoplasmic reticulum, and mitochondria. One of the most useful applications of organelle isolation is to determine the localization of proteins within cells. Proteomics analysis of mitochondria also needs pure and intact isolated mitochondria in the first step. The quantity of isolated mitochondria could be determined by assessment of total protein content and mtDNA copy number.

**Methods:** Mitochondrial isolation from the human liver cell line (HepG2) was performed using two commercially available kits, including Qproteome (Qiagen) and MITOISO2 (Sigma-Aldrich), as well as a manual method. The integrity of the inner membrane of mitochondria was assessed by JC-1 staining. The activity of isolated mitochondria was evaluated by DCFH-DA staining. The total yield of isolated mitochondria was determined by the micro-Lowry method. Relative quantification using Real-time PCR was conducted to compare the mtDNA copy number of mitochondria isolated by the three different methods.

**Results:** Compared to other methods, the manual kit resulted in higher yields of the total amount of mitochondrial protein and mtDNA copy numbers. Isolated mitochondria by the Qproteome kit showed higher activity. Finally, the integrity of the inner membrane of isolated mitochondria was significantly higher in Qproteome when compared with the other two methods.

**Conclusion:** Due to differences in quality, quantity, and activity of isolated mitochondria using the three techniques discussed here, the method best-suited to each research project should be selected according to the distinct features of isolated mitochondria.

**Keywords:** Mitochondrial isolation, Cell line, HepG2

**Steps for Mitochondrial Isolation:**
1. Mitochondrial isolation from the human liver cell line (HepG2) was performed using two commercially available kits, including Qproteome (Qiagen) and MITOISO2 (Sigma-Aldrich), as well as a manual method.
2. The integrity of the inner membrane of mitochondria was assessed by JC-1 staining.
3. The activity of isolated mitochondria was evaluated by DCFH-DA staining.
4. The total yield of isolated mitochondria was determined by the micro-Lowry method.
5. Relative quantification using Real-time PCR was conducted to compare the mtDNA copy number of mitochondria isolated by the three different methods.

**Notes:**
- The article does not provide detailed step-by-step instructions for each method.
- The article does not mention the specific steps involved in the manual method.
- The article does not provide information on the duration or temperature of the isolation process.
- The article does not mention any centrifugation steps or specific buffers used in the isolation process.

**Conclusion:**
The article compares three methods for mitochondrial isolation from the human liver cell line (HepG2). The methods evaluated include two commercially available kits (Qproteome and MITOISO2) and a manual method. The article reports differences in quality, quantity

 Using last summary
Protocol: **Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Mitochondrial isolation was performed using three available methods, including two commercial kits and one manual method.
2. The commercial kits used were the Qproteum mitochondria isolation kit (Qiagen, USA) and the MITOISO2 mitochondria isolation kit (Sigma, USA).
3. The manual isolation method was performed using differential centrifugation as previously described by Dhruv Sareen, et al. with some modifications.
4. In the homogenization step, a dounce homogenizer was used to have a gentler treatment with mitochondria exerted from cells.
5. The extraction buffer used was the same for all methods.
6. The activity assay was performed using a respiratory buffer described by Mashayekhi et al., which includes MOPS, MgCl2, Sodium succinate, and EGTA.
7. The respiratory buffer includes KH2PO4 instead of KCl.
8. The quantity of isolated mitochondria was assessed using total protein content as a marker.
9. The Biuret and Lowry methods were used to assess the micrograms of isolated mitochondrial protein.
10. The micro-Lowry method with Onishi and Barr Modification (TP0200, Sigma-Aldrich, USA) was used according to the manufacturer's instructions.
11. The absorbance of the colored solution at 500 nm and 800 nm was read to obtain raw data.
12. A standard curve was drawn using a 400-µg/mL stock diluted in water into 50, 100, 200, and 300 µg/mL aliquots.
13. The protein concentration of each sample was determined, and the results were multiplied by the dilution factor to obtain the total protein concentration of isolated mitochondria using each method.
14. Relative mtDNA copy numbers of isolated mitochondria were measured using a SYBR green based quantitative real-time PCR assay.
15. Primers for detection and amplification of mtDNA were designed for the tRNA Leu (UUR) gene.
16. All tests were repeated three times, conducted in triplicate.
17. β-2-microglobulin was used as an endogenous control for normalization of relative quantification test results.
18. The ROX fluorescent dye was added to the real-time PCR master mix as a background dye to normalize the fluorescent signals in each reaction.
19. DNA was extracted using the QIAamp DNA mini kit (Qiagen, USA) with some modifications.
20. RNase A treatment was eliminated to avoid the negative effect on mtDNA yield.
21. All PCR procedures were performed using RNase Free material and plastic-ware.

**Incompleteness:**
- The article does not provide specific details about the steps involved in the differential centrifugation method.
- The article does not provide specific details about the modifications made to the manual isolation method.
- The article does not provide specific details about the SYBR green based quantitative real-time PCR assay.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed description of the mitochondrial isolation steps using three different methods: two commercial kits and one manual method. However, there are some protocol gaps and missing details, particularly regarding the differential centrifugation method, modifications to the manual isolation method, and the SYBR green based quantitative real-time PCR assay. Additional information would be needed to fully replicate the procedures.

Protocol: **Steps for Mitochondrial Isolation:**

1. "Measurement of total protein content of isolated mitochondria (from 10^6 cultured cells) using micro-Lowry method revealed that manual differential centrifugation method provides more mitochondria (58.03 µg) than the MITOISO2 kit (43.9 µg). Among these methods, Qproteum kit (37.6 µg) had the lowest yield." (Azimzadeh et al., 2016)

2. "Relative mtDNA copy number calculation revealed that mtDNA content of manual method was 3.4 fold and 2.2 fold higher than Qproteome and MITOISO2 kits, respectively." (Azimzadeh et al., 2016)

3. "Comparison of mitochondrial membrane integrity was performed using JC-1 staining and showed Qproteome kit provides more intact mitochondria and the isolated mitochondria by manual method had the lowest rank of membrane integrity." (Azimzadeh et al., 2016)

4. "Results of mitochondrial activity assays showed that mitochondria isolated using the manual method and the MITOISO2 kit have quite similar activities. Mitochondria isolated with Qproteome showed a statistically significant high fluorescent intensity when compared to other methods." (Azimzadeh et al., 2016)

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**

- The article does not provide a detailed step-by-step protocol for each method of mitochondrial isolation.
- The specific centrifugation speeds, times, and temperatures are not mentioned.
- The specific reagents and buffers used in each method are not described.
- The article does not mention the specific steps for cell lysis or homogenization.

**Conclusion:**

The article provides a comparison of three methods for mitochondrial isolation, but it does not provide a complete protocol for each method. The steps mentioned highlight the differences in total protein content, mtDNA copy number, membrane integrity, and mitochondrial activity between the methods. However, the protocol gaps make it difficult to replicate the experiments without additional information.

Protocol: **Steps for Mitochondrial Isolation:**

1. "To the best of our knowledge, there are limited studies regarding the comparison of different methods for mitochondrial isolation... Accordingly, we did not find any articles that compared such methods for isolation of mitochondria from human liver tissue or cell lines."

2. "Several other studies have isolated the mitochondria from tissues and cells using manual methods consisted of sequential steps of homogenization, washing and centrifugation in 600g to 11000g."

3. "Added to these data, several studies have been used kit-based method for isolation of mitochondria especially for further injection strategies."

4. "Main differences among three studied methods for mitochondrial isolation were in time efficiency, cost, equipment needed, quality and quantity of isolated mitochondria."

**Conclusion:**

The article mentions that there are limited studies comparing different methods for mitochondrial isolation from human liver tissue or cell lines. However, it does mention that several studies have used manual methods consisting of sequential steps of homogenization, washing, and centrifugation. Additionally, kit-based methods have been used for mitochondrial isolation, especially for injection strategies. The article highlights that the main differences among the studied methods are in time efficiency, cost, equipment needed, and the quality and quantity of isolated mitochondria. Protocol: **Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Sonication of a suspension of rat liver cells for 10-30 s yields a homogenate.
2. Tightly coupled mitochondria can be isolated from the homogenate.
3. The yield of mitochondrial protein is approximately 100-120 mg starting from 5 g of liver tissue.
4. The mitochondrial fraction is essentially free of contaminating plasma membrane and microsomes and contains only small amounts of peroxisomes and lysosomes.
5. Hepatocytes are isolated from rat liver tissue using collagenase from Clostridium histolyticum.
6. The plasma membrane of isolated hepatocytes is disrupted by sonication.
7. The sonication method is suitable for the isolation of highly coupled mitochondria and other subcellular organelles from hepatocytes.

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The specific duration of sonication is not provided (e.g., 10-30 s, but no exact time is mentioned).
- The concentration of collagenase used is not mentioned.
- The specific method for liberating and filtering the cells through nylon filters is not described in detail.
- The specific method for determining the viability of hepatocytes is not mentioned.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed method for the isolation of intact mitochondria from isolated rat liver cells. The steps mentioned include sonication of liver cell suspension, isolation of tightly coupled mitochondria, and the absence of contaminating plasma membrane and microsomes. However, there are some protocol gaps and uncertainties regarding the duration of sonication, collagenase concentration, cell liberation and filtering method, and viability determination of hepatocytes.

Error in summarizing article: Text cannot be empty
 Using last summary
Protocol: 1. Mitochondria were isolated from 200 mg of isolated rat liver cells.
2. The cells were sonicated for 10-30 seconds to disrupt them.
3. The disrupted cells were removed by centrifugation at 480g for 10 minutes.
4. The resulting supernatant was centrifuged at 4300g for 20 minutes to isolate the mitochondria.
5. The pellet containing the mitochondria was washed twice in 20 ml of 0.25 M sucrose at 4300g for 15 minutes.
6. The purity of the mitochondrial preparation was determined by assaying several marker enzymes, including rotenone insensitive NADPH-cytochrome c reductase, urate oxidase, acid phosphatase, Y-AMP phosphatase, cytochrome oxidase, and monoamine oxidase.
7. The yield of mitochondrial protein obtained by the sonication procedure was approximately 100-120 mg from a 5-g liver.
8. Mitochondria isolated after 10-30 seconds of sonication showed high respiratory control ratios and ADP:O ratios.
9. The mitochondria isolated after sonication were sensitive to FCCP, rotenone, antimycin, cyanide, and oligomycin.
10. The mitochondrial fraction obtained after 30 seconds of sonication was virtually free of plasma membrane and microsomes, and contained approximately 27% of the lysosomal and peroxisomal fractions.
11. The sonication procedure described is a rapid and simple method for the preparation of mitochondria from isolated rat liver cells, with a high degree of intactness.
12. The yield and purity of the mitochondria obtained by sonication are comparable to those obtained by homogenization of intact liver tissue.
13. The method can potentially be applied to the isolation of other subcellular components from isolated hepatocytes.
14. The procedure minimizes the handling of radioactive material when incubating hepatocytes with radioactive material.

**Conclusion:** The article provides a detailed protocol for the isolation of mitochondria from isolated rat liver cells using sonication. The protocol includes steps for cell disruption, removal of disrupted cells, centrifugation to isolate mitochondria, washing of the mitochondrial pellet, and assessment of mitochondrial purity. However, some details are missing, such as the specific sonication conditions (e.g., power, frequency), the composition of the washing solution, and the specific marker enzyme assays used for purity determination.

Protocol: **Article:** Mitochondrial Regulation: Methods and Protocols, Second Edition

**Section:** 1 Isolation of Mitochondria from Liver and Extraction of Total RNA and Protein: Analyses of miRNA and Protein Expression

**Steps:**

1. Isolate mitochondria from liver.
2. Extract total RNA and protein from the isolated mitochondria.
3. Analyze miRNA expression using the extracted RNA.
4. Analyze protein expression using the extracted protein.

**Section:** 2 Determination of Oxidative Phosphorylation Complexes Activities

**Steps:**

1. Perform the isolation of mitochondria.
2. Measure the activities of oxidative phosphorylation complexes in the isolated mitochondria.

**Section:** 3 BN-PAGE-Based Approach to Study Thyroid Hormones and Mitochondrial Function

**Steps:**

1. Isolate mitochondria.
2. Perform blue native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (BN-PAGE) to study thyroid hormones and mitochondrial function.

**Section:** 4 Monitoring Mitochondrial Function in Mouse Embryonic Stem Cells (mESCs)

**Steps:**

1. Monitor mitochondrial function in mouse embryonic stem cells.
2. Use fluorescent probes to assess mitochondrial function.

**Section:** 5 Mitochondrial Functional Assessment in Mammalian Gametes Using Fluorescent Probes

**Steps:**

1. Assess mitochondrial function in mammalian gametes.
2. Use fluorescent probes for the assessment.

**Conclusion:**

The article provides several methods for studying mitochondrial regulation. The steps for isolating mitochondria from liver and extracting total RNA and protein are mentioned, but the detailed protocols are not provided. The steps for determining oxidative phosphorylation complexes activities, studying thyroid hormones and mitochondrial function using BN-PAGE, and monitoring mitochondrial function in mouse embryonic stem cells and mammalian gametes are described without any missing information. Overall, the article provides a comprehensive overview of various methods for studying mitochondrial regulation, but more detailed protocols are needed for some of the mentioned steps.

Protocol: **Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. Use a speed-controlled mechanical drill (Tri-R model K41 skill drill) for the isolation process.
2. Use a tissue grinder with a glass mortar and radially serrated PTFE pestle for further processing.
3. Prepare an ultraspin buffer containing 0.25 M sucrose and 1 mM ethylene glycol-bis (2-aminoethylether)-N,N,N0,N0-tetraacetic acid (EGTA). Mix 21.39 g of sucrose and 95.1 mg of EGTA in 150 ml of water. Adjust the pH to 7.4 with KOH and complete the volume to 250 mL with water.
4. Use Percoll, a colloidal PVP-coated silica, for cell separation during the isolation process.
5. Prepare a homogenate buffer containing 70 mM sucrose, 220 mM mannitol, 1 mM EGTA, and 10 mM 4-(2-Hydroxyethyl)piperazine-1-ethanesulfonic acid, N-(2-Hydroxyethyl) piperazine-N-0-(2-ethanesulfonic acid) (HEPES), pH 7.4. Add 700 mL of water to a glass beaker.

**Incompleteness:**
- The article does not provide the complete protocol for mitochondrial isolation. It only mentions the materials and reagents used.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a brief overview of the materials and reagents used for mitochondrial isolation, but it does not provide a complete protocol with step-by-step instructions. Further information is needed to fully understand the mitochondrial isolation process.

Protocol: Steps for Mitochondria Isolation:

1. Weigh 23.6 g sucrose, 40.8 g mannitol, 380.4 mg EGTA, and 2.38 g HEPES and transfer to the cylinder.
2. Mix and adjust pH to 7.4 with KOH.
3. Adjust volume to 1 L.
4. Prepare Mitochondria Wash Buffer 1: Add 800 mL of water to a glass beaker.
5. Weigh 7.46 g KCl, 1.05 g MOPS, and 0.380 mg EGTA and transfer to the glass beaker.
6. Mix and adjust pH to 7.4 with KOH.
7. Adjust volume to 1 L.
8. Prepare Mitochondria Wash Buffer 2: Add 800 mL of water to a glass beaker.
9. Weigh 7.46 g KCl and 1.05 g MOPS and transfer to the glass beaker.
10. Mix and adjust pH to 7.4 with KOH.
11. Adjust volume to 1 L.
12. Prepare Mitochondria Suspension Buffer: Add 70 mL of water to a glass beaker.
13. Weigh 4.28 g sucrose, 372.8 mg KCl, 119.15 mg HEPES, and 27 mg KH2PO4 and transfer to the glass beaker.
14. Mix and adjust volume to 100 mL.
15. Use EDTA-Free Complete®-Mini Protease Inhibitor Cocktail for protease inhibition.
16. Use Chelex ™100 Resin for RNA extraction.
17. Use RNAse A in 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 8.0), 10 mM EDTA for RNA extraction.

Conclusion:
The steps provided describe the process of mitochondria isolation. However, there are some missing protocol parts, such as the specific procedure for liver fragment preparation and the details of using the ultraspin buffer and percoll for gradient preparation.

Error in summarizing article: Text cannot be empty
 Using last summary
Protocol: 1. Homogenize the samples as a 10% (wt/vol) homogenate in homogenate buffer with protease inhibitor using 6-10 up & down strokes with a speed-controlled mechanical drill and tissue grinder at 800 rpm.
2. Centrifuge at 600 xg for 10 min at 4ºC. Collect the supernatant and discard the pellet.
3. Centrifuge the supernatant at 1,100 xg for 10 min at 4ºC. Collect the supernatant and discard the pellet.
4. Centrifuge the supernatant at 7,700 xg for 10 min at 4ºC. Collect the pellet and discard the supernatant.
5. Resuspend the pellet in homogenate buffer with protease inhibitor.
6. Carefully and slowly layer the resuspended pellet on top of a self-generating gradient.
7. Centrifuge at 43,000 xg for 1 h at 4ºC. Collect the lower darker mitochondrial band.
8. Resuspend the collected mitochondrial band in mitochondria wash buffer 1.
9. Centrifuge at 7,700 xg for 10 min at 4ºC. Discard the supernatant and keep the pellet.
10. Wash the purified mitochondria twice by resuspending in Chelex-100-treated mitochondria wash buffer 2.
11. Centrifuge at 7,700 xg for 10 min at 4ºC. Collect the pellet and discard the supernatant.
12. Resuspend the pellet in Chelex-100-treated mitochondria suspension buffer.
13. Add 2 mg of RNAse A per mL of the resuspended pellet and incubate for 1 h at 37ºC.
14. Centrifuge at 12,000 xg for 3 min at 4ºC. Collect the pellet and discard the supernatant.
15. Wash the mitochondria pellet twice by resuspending in Chelex TM 100-treated mitochondria suspension buffer.
16. Centrifuge at 12,000 xg for 3 min at 4ºC. Collect the mitochondrial pellet and discard the supernatant.
17. Proceed to RNA extraction or store the pellet at -80ºC.

**Incompleteness:** The article does not provide details on the preparation of buffers or the collection of the liver sample.

**Conclusion:** The protocol for mitochondrial isolation is mostly complete, with all the necessary steps mentioned. However, there are some missing details regarding the preparation of buffers and the collection of the liver sample.

Protocol: 1. Centrifuge the mitochondrial suspension at 12,000 x g for 3 min at 4°C.
2. Discard the supernatant and keep the purified mitochondrial pellet on ice.
3. Wash the mitochondrial pellet two times by resuspending in Chelex™100-treated mitochondrial suspension buffer and centrifuging at 12,000 x g for 3 min at 4°C.
4. Discard the supernatant and place the purified mitochondria on ice.
5. Add TRIzol™ to the purified mitochondria pellet and homogenize the sample by pipetting up and down. Incubate for 5 min at room temperature.
6. Add 0.2 mL of chloroform per mL of TRIzol™ reagent used for sample homogenization. Shake vigorously by hand for 15 s, followed by 3 min incubation at room temperature.
7. Centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 15 min at 4°C to allow phase separation and collect the upper aqueous phase into a new tube.
8. Save the interphase and lower phenol-chloroform phase at 4°C or -80°C for subsequent total protein extraction.
9. Precipitate the RNA by adding 0.5 mL of isopropanol per mL of TRIzol™ reagent used for sample homogenization to the aqueous phase containing the RNA. Shake by hand for 15 s and incubate at room temperature for 10 min.
10. Centrifuge at 12,000 x g for 10 min at 4°C and discard the supernatant.
11. Wash the RNA pellet by adding 1 mL of 75% ethanol per mL of TRIzol™ reagent used for sample homogenization and mix the tube by inversion ten times.
12. Centrifuge at 7,500 x g for 5 min at 4°C and discard the supernatant.
13. Air dry the RNA pellet for 5 min.
14. Resuspend the RNA in DNAse/RNAse-free water.
15. Proceed to downstream applications or store the RNA at -80°C.

**Protocol Completeness:** The protocol for mitochondrial isolation and total RNA extraction is provided in detail. However, there are no specific steps mentioned for the initial mitochondrial isolation from liver or the preparation of the Chelex™100-treated mitochondrial suspension buffer. Additionally, there is no information on the subsequent total protein extraction from TRIzol™-treated isolated mitochondria.
